topic: Two friends both apply for the same job
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed with an unbearable pitch, vibrating right behind Elias's eyes. He stared at the application form, the ink blurring into meaningless squiggles. *It can’t be. It absolutely cannot be.* His hands, slick with nervous sweat, trembled so violently he had to grip the edge of the desk to keep from dropping the sheaf of papers. He kept re-reading the job description, searching for some hidden clause, some typo that would invalidate everything. A strange, hot pressure built behind his sternum, a frantic drumming that made his breath catch in shallow, sharp gasps. He glanced over at Marcus, who was calmly reviewing his own submission, a faint, almost amused smile playing on his lips. Elias felt a sudden, overwhelming urge to bolt, to crumple the entire document and throw it into the nearest wastebasket, just to break the suffocating reality of the situation.

[story 2]

“Are you sure about this, Leo?” Sarah asked, her voice thin and reedy, as she watched him pace the worn carpet of the waiting room. Leo didn't look at her. He was tracing the outline of the job title on his application with a fingertip, his jaw tight enough to crack. Every sound in the room—the distant phone ringing, the squeak of a chair—seemed amplified, grating against his nerves. A dizzying sensation washed over him, making the room tilt slightly. *This is a trap. A cruel joke.* He squeezed his eyes shut, trying to force the frantic energy churning in his gut down, but it only surged, a wild, uncontrollable tide threatening to pull him under. When he finally looked up, his pupils were blown wide, and he could feel the heat rising in his neck, a prickly, overwhelming need to scream into the void.

[story 3]

"It's impossible," whispered Ben, his voice tight with disbelief, as he stared at the identical lines of text on both their respective resumes. His hands were clasped so tightly his knuckles were white, straining against the pressure. He began to shake his head, a rapid, jerky motion that seemed to shake the very foundations of his composure. A high-pitched ringing filled his ears, drowning out rational thought. He felt a profound sense of wrongness, a sickening lurch in his stomach that made him nauseous. He started pacing back and forth, his movements becoming jerky and undirected, his gaze darting everywhere and nowhere. He muttered under his breath, a ragged sound, a desperate plea for the universe to just let this absurdity cease existing.
